query: Crew reacting to a TCAS RA while configuring to land or on short finals.
Ground proximity GPWS warnings triggered when losing altitude or navigating above topographical peaks.
Sourcing certified mechanics to swap out fractured first-stage rotor components on a CFM56-7B.
Disposing of PW4000 hardware because the securing fasteners had sheared off.
Unmanned operators assuming they possessed LAANC clearance when the digital permission was actually lapsed, unfiled, or geographically incorrect.
Cruising at FL340 when the aviators detect a catastrophic powerplant failure accompanied by a concussive sound.
Flying an ILS approach and intercepting the localizer but suffering from guidance tracking dropouts or sudden lateral automated swings.
When lateral automation like LNAV incorrectly locks onto ghost signals and triggers aggressive, uncommanded bank angles to correct.
A Cessna encountering a total generator or breaker trip at night, stripping the aviator of external comms and interior illumination.
Certified Part 107 pilots accidentally crossing into restricted boundaries because they relied on outdated third-party geospatial apps.
Dispatching a ship with an MEL, but the technicians improperly secured the bypasses or left panels compromised, causing inflight consequences.
ATC instructing a drop to FL180, but the flight crew had to arrest the descent early to dodge an unannounced target climbing into their block.
Aviators fat-fingering the destination or intermediate intersection into the flight management computer due to phonetically identical geographical naming conventions.
An airport worker declining to obey management instructions to wipe up an unidentified liquid leaking from a priority package, citing union rules and lack of protective gear.
The flight deck registering an inability to maintain cabin altitude during climb because technicians failed to properly seal an external access point after dealing with a deferred defect.
Rubber shredding from the undercarriage during the deceleration phase, forcing the pilot to pull onto unpaved areas or resulting in underside structural damage.
Pilots initiating block-out after testing a powerplant fluid issue on the ramp, but forgetting to obtain the finalized digital sign-off from the technical team.

A complete loss of the ship's generated power after dark, leaving the aviator with only a flashlight for panel illumination and requiring them to use a mobile network to communicate with the aerodrome.
Sanitation protocols involving harsh chemicals causing data input peripherals at a radar control center to malfunction or stick, delaying handoffs.
A teacher resorting to hitting the learner in the cockpit to regain authority over the control column just as the aircraft was trying to unstick from the tarmac.
The pilot monitoring failing to catch that the flight guidance computer bypassed a vertical waypoint constraint because they were utilizing a different thrust/pitch logic to slow down.
Customers inadvertently or intentionally stowing high-capacity energy storage cells in the cargo hold, bypassing the standard gate screening for restricted electronic items.
Ground vehicles reversing multiple airliners into a confined tarmac space simultaneously, resulting in gridlock and dangerous proximity between tail sections.
Department of Defense assets exiting their designated training polygons unannounced and encroaching on civil airways without prior coordination.
Avian ingestion at the exact moment of becoming airborne, leading the cockpit crew to keep the undercarriage extended while dealing with powerplant shaking.
Lighter-than-air craft encountering sudden meteorological shifts near the surface, causing the canopy to drape over residential structures or heavy machinery.
Aviators ending up on incorrect or closed pavement surfaces because the asphalt lines were barely visible or the navigational placards were inadequate.
Non-aircraft tarmac traffic ignoring right-of-way rules at intersections, compelling the flight deck to perform harsh stops to prevent a ground collision.
The second-in-command losing consciousness and collapsing in the front preparation area, forcing the captain to handle the remainder of the high-altitude transit solo.

A traveler suffering from acute gastrointestinal sickness mid-flight, prompting the cabin staff to clear the aft area, originally feared to be a highly contagious virus but later attributed to over-imbibing.
